aliases: Golden Chick Bites EC Coffee Bar

english: Golden Chick Bites EC Coffee Bar

food_subgroup: Fried chicken pieces / poultry snack bites

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Malaysian-style golden fried chicken bites; closest parent 'fried chicken food product' (FOODON_00002670) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for battered/breaded fried chicken bites; 'Chicken meat, cooked' (A00LC) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226803007 'Fried chicken (food)' is the closest available term; no specific code for bite-sized Malaysian fried chicken pieces.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Golden Chick Bites consists of approximately 6 to 8 bite-sized fried chicken pieces weighing around 150 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken, battered, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05062)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for battered fried chicken (NDB 05062 / FDC 171477). Malaysian-style golden chick bites are comparable in composition to battered deep-fried chicken pieces. IMR Malaysia MyFCD does not contain a specific entry for this product. Values are representative of battered deep-fried chicken and are used as the best available approved-source estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate foods — fried chicken analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malaysian golden chick bites. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous battered fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Battered fried chicken has a moderate-to-low GI due to the protein and fat content slowing glucose absorption; the wheat-flour batter contributes carbohydrate but the overall GI remains low. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (45 × 11.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.6 g.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of Golden Chick Bites, as a 150 g serving provides approximately 585 mg sodium, representing about 25% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; pairing with low-sodium vegetables and avoiding additional dipping sauces is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat-flour batter contributing moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Golden Chick Bites have a low glycemic index (estimated GI 45) due to the protein and fat content moderating glucose absorption, but the battered coating contributes approximately 16.8 g of carbohydrate per 150 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should account for this carbohydrate load within their meal plan and limit deep-fried foods to occasional consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and deep-frying oil absorption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 150 g serving of Golden Chick Bites provides approximately 5.4 g of saturated fat and 20.4 g of total fat from deep-frying; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of deep-fried foods and consider grilled or baked chicken alternatives to reduce saturated fat and total fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Golden Chick Bites are bite-sized pieces of chicken that have been coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. This dish is served at Malaysian-style coffee bars and casual dining establishments in Singapore, such as EC Coffee Bar on Carpenter Street. The exterior is crunchy and golden-brown, while the interior remains juicy and tender. The seasoning typically includes garlic, pepper, salt, and aromatic spices characteristic of Malaysian cooking. Nutritionally, a 150 g serving provides approximately 369 kcal, 29.7 g protein, 20.4 g fat, and 16.8 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is moderate to high at approximately 585 mg per serving, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making the carbohydrate impact moderate when consumed in a typical portion. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains gluten and egg allergens. It is considered halal. Clinically, the deep-fried preparation method and saturated fat content make this a food to be consumed in moderation by individuals with cardiovascular disease or Type 2 Diabetes, though the high protein content supports satiety.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Golden Chick Bites?
Is Golden Chick Bites suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of fried chicken bites?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Golden Chick Bites exists in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy battered fried chicken (FDC 171477), which is the closest approved-source match. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because battered deep-fried chicken is a well-characterised food category and the adaptation is defensible.
notes: Vitamin C is 0.0 mg per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for battered fried chicken as the deep-frying process destroys heat-labile vitamin C and chicken meat contains negligible vitamin C. All -1 sentinel values (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) have been replaced with numeric values sourced from USDA FDC 171477 where available. No sentinel -1 values remain in this record. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous battered fried chicken from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (45 × 11.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.6 g. The price of SGD 26.30 from field survey data is noted but not used as a nutritional data point. This item is served by EC Coffee Bar - Carpenter Street on field survey data. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as this is a Malaysian dish served in Singapore; the Chinese name 黄金鸡块 (golden chicken pieces) is a direct semantic translation of the dish name.
